policy_statement: Complete and implement the Stormwater Management Master Plan.
mechanism_description: Complete and implement a stormwater management master plan to improve stormwater handling, reducing flooding impacts and protecting water quality.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme precipitation) → stormwater system upgrades/operations → ↓ runoff peak + ↑ infiltration/storage → ↓ flood exposure/impacts (+ improved receiving-water quality)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Municipal planning, design, and implementation of stormwater controls (e.g., pipes/culverts, detention/retention, green infrastructure, maintenance regimes, outfall controls) guided by a master plan.  
2) Mediating process: Hydrologic modification that attenuates peak flows, increases infiltration and temporary storage, reduces combined sewer/storm overflow frequency, and improves pollutant capture/settling/filtration.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced flooding frequency/severity and associated damages/service disruption under intensified rainfall (adaptation), with ancillary benefits of improved water quality and ecosystem/health protection.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood damages and disruption by lowering runoff peaks and improving stormwater conveyance/storage during heavier rainfall events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Managing heavier rainfall/runoff to reduce flood exposure and impacts—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While water-quality protection and possible energy/GHG co-benefits can occur, the most direct and largest climate-relevant impact across policies of this mechanism is risk reduction from precipitation-driven flooding.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented mainly as green infrastructure, it can also modestly sequester carbon or reduce energy for pumping/treatment, but these are typically secondary to flood resilience and water-quality objectives.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any “stormwater management master plan implementation” necessarily focuses on stormwater capacity, infiltration/storage, and runoff control, which predominantly serves flood-risk reduction under climate change.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: Secondary subtypes (e.g., nature-based vs grey infrastructure, water-quality emphasis, combined sewer overflow control) require policy-specific evidence and are deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a jurisdiction’s master plan is framed chiefly around regulatory water-quality compliance (e.g., pollutant load reductions) with minimal flood-risk component, classification could lean toward Resource Efficiency/other environmental outcomes; however, for stormwater master plans the dominant climate link is typically flood adaptation.
co_benefits: Improved receiving-water quality; reduced pollutant loading/overflows; potential urban greening/amenity benefits if green infrastructure is included.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Implement” could imply capital works, but the text does not specify particular projects or spending; therefore classified as a planning/strategy instrument rather than infrastructure investment.
